context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — SWOT Analysis

Recommended due date: August 15 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigation.

This assignment will be reviewed by course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with fellow learners for peer learning opportunities.

Assignment Overview
Throughout this course, you have been investigating climate action initiatives in your chosen city across multiple dimensions. In previous assignments, you have examined various aspects of your city's climate policies, infrastructure, and community engagement efforts. This week, you will synthesize your findings by conducting a comprehensive SWOT (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, and Threats) Analysis that evaluates your city's climate action potential across all Urban Pathways.

Understanding SWOT Analysis
SWOT Analysis is a strategic planning tool that helps identify internal and external factors that can impact an organization's or, in this case, a city's ability to achieve its goals. For climate action planning, SWOT analysis provides a structured framework to assess:
• Strengths: Internal positive factors that give the city advantages in addressing climate change (existing infrastructure, policies, resources, capabilities)
• Weaknesses: Internal negative factors that hinder climate action (resource limitations, policy gaps, infrastructure deficits)
• Opportunities: External positive factors that the city could leverage for climate action (funding sources, partnerships, emerging technologies, policy changes)
• Threats: External negative factors that could impede progress (economic pressures, political changes, natural disasters, competing priorities)

Assignment Instructions
• Re-evaluate your Spider Diagram. Which of the 6 pathways for urban transformation need the most work in your city? Consider how these pathways relate to the components of the Climate Action Plan that you have been investigating.
• Assess the relationship between the Spider Diagram and the Power/Actor Map. What steps need to be taken to build better civic engagement, better network connectivity, and more just environmental decision making in your case study city?
• Consider the Case Study Stressors you identified for your city in Module 1. Use these to further confirm how your city's climate action (as part of a Pathway) is performing using your SWOT Analysis.
• Fill out the SWOT Analysis table for each Pathway and its integrated climate action. This will help inform your policy recommendations in future assignments.

See SWOT Analysis Table Below

Completion Guidelines
For each cell in the table:
• Provide 2-3 specific, concrete examples
• Include relevant data, policies, or initiatives where possible
• Consider both current conditions and recent developments
• Think about how factors in one pathway might influence others
• Reference your previous assignments (Spider Diagram, Power/Actor Map, Case Study Stressors)

Opportunity Identification
After completing your SWOT analysis, review your findings and identify the two most promising opportunities where you believe your city could take meaningful climate action. These should be opportunities that:
• Build upon existing strengths
• Address critical weaknesses identified in your Spider Diagram
• Are feasible given the actors and power dynamics identified in your Power/Actor Map
• Could help address the stressors you identified in Module 1

Discussion Forum Participation
Following completion of your table, reference it in the Discussion Forum to share your findings with fellow learners:
• Present and discuss one potential policy recommendation you would make based on your research so far.
• Why is this recommendation important for your city? Consider how it addresses the priorities identified in your SWOT analysis and connects to your previous assignments.

Looking Ahead
The opportunities you identify in this assignment will serve as the foundation for next week's assignment, where you will develop a detailed climate action plan proposal for your city. Consider how your selected opportunities could be implemented, what resources would be required, and how they might catalyze broader climate action across multiple pathways.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m working on the SWOT analysis for my city climate case study, and I’m a little unsure how to turn my earlier Spider Diagram and Power/Actor Map into actual SWOT points. Could you help me figure out a good way to organize that without jumping straight to final answers?
exchanges.tutor: Great starting point! Before we map anything over, let me ask you this: in SWOT, Strengths and Weaknesses are *internal* to the city, while Opportunities and Threats are *external*. With that in mind, which of your two earlier tools — the Spider Diagram or the Power/Actor Map — do you think speaks more to internal factors, and which might point more toward external ones? What's your instinct?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a general methodological question about connecting their previous work to the SWOT framework. This is a good starting point. Rather than explaining the process to them, I should ask a Socratic question that gets them to think about the relationship between the Spider Diagram and the internal/external distinction in SWOT. I want to keep it bite-sized and let them do the thinking.

exchanges.student: I think the Spider Diagram feels more internal because it shows how strong or weak the city already is across the pathways. The Power/Actor Map seems kind of mixed to me, though, because some actors are inside city government and some are outside, so I’m not totally sure where to place that.
exchanges.tutor: Your instinct about the Spider Diagram is spot on. And your hesitation about the Power/Actor Map shows good critical thinking. So let me push on that a bit: in the SWOT framework, what do you think makes a factor 'external'? Is it just about whether an actor is inside or outside city government, or could there be another way to draw that line?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a solid observation about the Spider Diagram mapping to internal factors, and is genuinely wrestling with the mixed nature of the Power/Actor Map. This is good thinking. Rather than resolving the ambiguity for them, I should affirm the valid reasoning and push them to think more precisely about what makes an actor 'internal' vs 'external' in the context of SWOT — this will help them self-resolve the confusion.

exchanges.student: I was first thinking “outside city government = external,” but that feels too simple. Maybe external means the city can’t directly control it, even if it still affects decisions a lot?
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly the right reframe — control is the key idea. Now apply that to your own Power/Actor Map: can you give me one example of an actor your city *can* directly control or influence, and one it largely *cannot*? What SWOT category would each fall into?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a more sophisticated and accurate understanding of the internal/external distinction — control vs. no control, rather than location. This is a key insight that will help them correctly categorize actors from their Power/Actor Map. I should affirm this and immediately push them to apply it concretely to their own map rather than staying abstract.

exchanges.student: I think a city department or mayor’s office would be something the city can directly influence, so that would connect more to strengths or weaknesses depending on how capable it is. A national government or maybe global energy prices would be less controllable, so those seem more like opportunities or threats.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — you've got the logic down. Now let's make it concrete. In your Spider Diagram, which pathway scored lowest for your city? That low score is likely pointing you toward a specific Weakness. What is it, and what's actually driving it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly applied the control framework to both internal and external categories, and has even noted that the SWOT category depends on whether the factor is positive or negative (capable vs. not capable, opportunity vs. threat). This is solid reasoning. Now I should push them to move from abstract examples to their actual case study city, connecting their Spider Diagram ratings to concrete SWOT points. This moves the conversation toward actual assignment work.

exchanges.student: I’m a bit stuck because I haven’t fully decided how to phrase the pathway names from my diagram into SWOT language. I think one of the lower areas was civic engagement / governance, but I’m not sure if the weakness should be “low participation,” “unequal decision-making,” or just “weak coordination.”
exchanges.tutor: Don't let the wording block you — the phrasing will follow from your evidence. Go back to your Power/Actor Map: what specifically did you observe about civic engagement in your city? Who's missing from the table, or where did you see coordination breaking down? Start there, and the right description will emerge naturally.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is overthinking phrasing and getting stuck in a minor detail — the difference between 'low participation,' 'unequal decision-making,' and 'weak coordination' is not something that needs to be resolved abstractly. This is a case where I should redirect them to their actual data and prior assignments rather than helping them find the 'right words.' The assignment asks for specific, concrete examples, so the phrasing will follow from the evidence, not the other way around.

exchanges.student: Okay, that helps. I noticed community-based groups had less formal power than city agencies and private developers, and frontline neighborhoods weren’t really centered in decision-making. So I guess the weakness is something like inequitable civic engagement or weak inclusion in climate governance?
exchanges.tutor: Good — that's grounded in your actual map, which is exactly right. Now flip it: does that same gap point toward any *Opportunity*? Think about external factors — funding, policy trends, outside organizations — that could help address that inclusion problem in your city's climate governance.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has grounded their weakness in concrete evidence from their Power/Actor Map — this is exactly what the assignment asks for. The framing they've arrived at is solid. I should affirm this briefly and then push them forward to think about whether this same observation points to an Opportunity (e.g., could strengthening community groups be an external lever?), which also connects to the assignment's 'Opportunity Identification' section. This keeps momentum going.

exchanges.student: Maybe an opportunity is outside grant funding or NGO partnerships that support participatory planning. I’m not fully sure if that’s specific enough, but it seems external and like it could help fix the inclusion gap.
exchanges.tutor: You're right to sense it needs more specificity — the assignment asks for concrete examples. Can you think of a real funding program, a specific NGO, or a policy trend that actually exists and applies to your city's context? What have you come across in your research so far?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track but hedging because they sense the answer lacks specificity. The assignment explicitly asks for concrete examples with relevant data, policies, or initiatives. Rather than validating the vague answer, I should push them to ground it in something real from their case study city — a specific funding program, a known NGO, or a policy trend.

exchanges.student: Yeah, that’s where I’m getting frustrated—I understand the categories, but I’m blanking on actual examples from my city research. Could you help me figure out how to pull one real example out of what I probably already have?
exchanges.tutor: Totally understandable — but the examples are in your own prior work! Go back to your Module 1 case study stressors or any sources you used earlier. What organizations, programs, or funding sources came up when you were researching your city's climate challenges? Even one name or initiative you remember would be a start.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing frustration and feeling stuck on recall, not on understanding. This is a good moment to be practical rather than purely Socratic — I should help them access their own prior work rather than giving them examples. I can prompt them to look back at their Module 1 stressors or previous assignments, and ask a specific question to trigger recall. I should not provide examples for them.

exchanges.student: I remember seeing resilience-related funding and some nonprofit involvement, but I can’t recall the exact program names right now. Could I start by listing the source or report where I saw them, and then turn that into an opportunity once I confirm the details?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's a sound approach — note the source and refine once confirmed. You now have enough to start drafting your SWOT table. You've identified at least one Weakness and one rough Opportunity for the civic engagement pathway. Why not start filling in those cells with what you have, and build from there? What pathway will you tackle first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is proposing a reasonable working approach — placeholder with source, then confirm. This is good academic practice and I should affirm it briefly. Then I should push them to actually move forward with the SWOT table rather than staying in planning mode. We're about 8 exchanges in and they have the conceptual framework down — time to encourage them to start filling in the table.

exchanges.student: I’ll start with the civic engagement/governance pathway since that’s the one I can describe most clearly right now. Before I draft it, can you quickly check if I’m thinking about this right: weakness = inequitable participation in climate decision-making, and opportunity = external funding/partners that could strengthen participatory planning?
exchanges.tutor: Your framing is solid — you've got the logic right. Now draft it. Remember the assignment asks for 2-3 concrete examples per cell, so as you write, push yourself to get specific. Go ahead and share what you come up with.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for validation before drafting — a reasonable check-in. Their framing is directionally correct. I should affirm briefly without over-praising, and push them to actually start writing rather than continuing to plan. We're near the end of our planned conversation length and they need to move into production mode. I should also remind them the assignment requires 2-3 specific concrete examples per cell, so their draft needs that specificity.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout all 10 exchanges. No submission-ready content was provided; the student generated all key ideas through guided questioning. Scaffolding was well-calibrated, building progressively from conceptual framing to concrete application. Responses were concise, supportive, and consistently anchored to the assignment.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave final answers or near-submission-ready content. Every key idea — the internal/external distinction, the control-based reframe, the weakness identification, the opportunity framing — was elicited from the student through targeted questions. No deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged and built on correct student progress (e.g., affirming the Spider Diagram instinct, the control reframe, the civic engagement weakness) and used diagnostic questions before redirecting (e.g., Turn 5 asking what the student observed rather than explaining). 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback, notably in Turn 7 explaining why specificity matters and directing the student back to their own research, and in Turn 9 validating the source-first approach as methodologically sound. 2.1–2.2: No repetitive cycling or off-topic drift occurred; the conversation advanced steadily. 3.1–3.2: Responses were appropriately brief and interactive, tone was warm and encouraging throughout, and no grading language was used.
